advantages: Does not require specifying cluster count k — the number of clusters emerges from eps, min_samples, and data density.
Finds arbitrarily shaped clusters (rings, chains, irregular blobs) that k-means centroids cannot represent well.
Explicit noise detection: sparse points labeled separately instead of being forced into the nearest cluster.
Deterministic given fixed data order and tie-breaking in standard implementations — reproducible with random_state where applicable.
Single scan plus neighborhood queries — no iterative centroid updates; often fast on moderate n with spatial indexes.
Interpretable parameters tied to physical scale when features are scaled: eps as a neighborhood radius in feature units.
disadvantages: Sensitive to eps and min_samples — poor choices yield one giant cluster, many noise points, or fragmented clusters.
Struggles when clusters have very different densities — one global eps cannot fit both tight and sparse groups (HDBSCAN addresses this elsewhere).
Feature scale and metric dominate neighbor counts — unscaled columns distort eps-neighborhoods without preprocessing.
High-dimensional data: distance concentration makes eps hard to interpret; performance and cluster quality often degrade.
Border point assignment can be order-dependent in some implementations when a point is border to multiple clusters.
Less interpretable summary than k-means centroids — clusters described by member sets, not a single representative vector.
